questions.text: I believe the world does not need more self-interested people—it needs more givers.
I find that my analytical engagement with painful situations has taught me things about effective helping that emotional engagement could not.
I would choose a lower-paying role that lets me serve my community over a higher-paying one that does not.
I rarely go back to past conversations, preferring to keep things current.
I feel that the moral demand of suffering is what gives ethical commitments their urgency and their meaning.
I believe that success comes from executing a clear vision with discipline.
I feel confident imposing boundaries when collaboration gets off track.
I tend to absorb stressful news without it producing significant physical symptoms.
I believe that the most equitable outcomes in situations involving human suffering are achieved by those who can evaluate needs without emotional distortion.
I routinely advocate for my own interests in negotiations and discussions.
I find that caffeine, lack of sleep, or other stressors amplify my emotional reactivity dramatically.
I hold the view that my car, my tools, and my space are shared assets for anyone who needs them.
I hold the view that the moral demand of suffering is not something to be balanced against other factors — it is the factor that sets the terms.
I tend to emerge from stressful periods without the accumulated tension that others describe.
I tend not to notice when someone's smile seems forced.
I detect when a group's energy shifts from casual to serious.
I think through the logical sequence of an argument before presenting it.
I tend to ask for the raise, the upgrade, or the better deal without hesitation.
I rarely detect when someone is performing agreement to keep the peace.
I sense when someone has rehearsed what they are about to say.
I find it energizing to direct a team through a challenging situation.
I do regular tasks when I remember them rather than relying on calendar prompts.
When a conversation stalls, I invite quieter voices to share before proposing anything.
I find that my tendency toward objective analysis during crises has repeatedly allowed me to identify the most efficient path forward.
I feel that a well-maintained system eventually runs itself, freeing my attention for new challenges.
I rarely experience the kind of emotional flooding that makes it hard to think.
I naturally step into the lead when a group seems directionless.
I find it important to stay aligned with the group, even at the cost of my personal preference.
I rarely notice the social dynamics of who pays for what in a group.
I experience the loss of a pet, a home, or a routine as grief that is every bit as intense as other forms of loss.
I prefer to let someone else set the direction before I contribute.
I experience creative inspiration as an almost feverish state.
I let my work time expand and contract naturally with my creative energy.
I notice the way someone's tone shifts slightly when they mention a particular person.
I find that eating alone at my favorite restaurant is a perfectly worthwhile way to spend an evening.
I believe that the moral priority of suffering is the principle that grounds ethical discourse in the real conditions of human existence.
